g.data-narration: Welcome to this lecture on Student's t-Distribution, a fundamental tool for statistical inference with small samples.
When estimating population mean, we often don't know the true standard deviation, forcing us to estimate it from sample data.
g.text: We want to estimate population mean μ but σ is unknown!
But σ is rarely known in practice!

g.data-narration: William Gosset developed the t-distribution to handle this exact problem, using sample standard deviation instead of population sigma.
g.text: William Sealy Gosset (1908) developed it under pseudonym "Student"
Substitute sample std dev s for unknown σ:
This new statistic follows Student's t-distribution!

g.data-narration: The t-distribution shape depends on degrees of freedom, calculated as n-1, which accounts for the extra uncertainty from estimation.
g.text: Shape is determined by: ν = n - 1

g.data-narration: The t-distribution is symmetric and bell-shaped like normal, but has heavier tails accounting for extra variability in small samples.
The t-statistic formula calculates how many standard errors the sample mean deviates from the hypothesized population mean.
Watch how the t-distribution with low degrees of freedom has much fatter tails compared to the standard normal curve.
We use t-distribution to construct confidence intervals for population mean when sigma is unknown, using the t-critical value.
g.text: When σ is unknown, CI for μ is:

g.data-narration: Let's work through a real example: measuring algorithm runtime across 16 datasets to estimate true mean performance.
g.text: Problem: Measure sorting algorithm runtime on 16 datasets.
Find 95% CI for true mean runtime μ.

g.data-narration: First we calculate degrees of freedom (n-1=15), then compute the standard error by dividing sample std dev by square root of n.
g.text: STEP 1: Calculate df and Standard Error

g.data-narration: For 95% confidence with 15 degrees of freedom, we look up t-critical value: t(0.025,15) equals 2.131 from the t-table.
The 95% confidence interval is 52 ± 2.66, giving us the range from 49.34 to 54.66 milliseconds for true mean runtime.
g.text: True μ is between 49.34 and 54.66 ms

g.data-narration: Beyond confidence intervals, the t-distribution enables hypothesis testing through one-sample t-tests comparing sample means to population values.
g.text: H₀: μ = μ₀ (test against hypothesized value)
Estimate population mean with known uncertainty
Real-world: A/B testing, clinical trials, algorithm comparison, machine learning experiments

g.data-narration: Remember these key points: use t when sigma is unknown, it has heavier tails than normal, and converges to Z for large samples.
The t-distribution is essential for statistical inference with small samples, forming the foundation for t-tests used across data science and research.
g.text: Student's t-distribution: The foundation for inference when σ is unknown
Essential for t-tests, confidence intervals, and analyzing small sample data in CS, engineering, and data science
